Career Advice 　>　 Cover Letters 　>　 Cover letter templates 　>　 Part-time cover letter template

Part-time cover letter template

by Michael Cheary

Struggling to find the right part-time role? The problem might be your cover letter…

Especially if you’re sending the same one for every application, or worse – not sending one at all. Think of it this way: your application letter for a part-time job is often the first thing a hiring manager sees – so spending a few extra minutes perfecting this part of your application can make all the difference in landing your next role.

We’ve already covered how to perfect your part-time CV, so to maximise your chances of impressing a recruiter, here’s how to write a cover letter for a part-time job (along with a free template to help you get started).

 

Free part-time cover letter template

Our part-time cover letter template can help you structure your thoughts and create a professional document. Download it below – and remember to adapt it for every job you apply for.

 

Part-Time Cover Letter Template

Please enter a valid email address

message here

By clicking Submit, you accept our Terms & Conditions and consent to receive emails and career related topics. See our Privacy policy for details. Continue without submitting

Download Part-Time Cover Letter Template

 

“A good part-time cover letter should highlight how well you manage your time, adapt to changing priorities, and make an impact within limited hours. Emphasise your flexibility, reliability, and any experience that proves you can balance responsibilities effectively. Tailor your message to the role, showing how you’ll add value from day one.”

 

Beth Green, Senior Executive Consultant | Reed

 

What is a cover letter for a part-time job?

A part-time job cover letter is a document you send with your CV to provide more information on your skills and experience. 

It’s your opportunity to explain why you’re interested in the role and why you’d be a great fit for the company. It’s a key part of any application, whether for a full-time or part-time position.

While a CV lists your qualifications and work history, a cover letter for a part-time position allows you to tell a story, connect with the employer on a personal level, and show your enthusiasm.

What is a cover letter?

Cover letter help

How to write a cover letter for a part-time job

A good application letter needs a clear structure to make it easy for recruiters to read. Follow this simple format to ensure your letter flows well and looks professional.

Contact information and employer details

Start by placing your contact information – name, address, phone number, and email – at the very top of the page. Below that, add the date you’re writing the letter. Then, include the employer’s details: the hiring manager’s name (if you can find it), their job title, the company name, and its address. 

Taking the time to address the letter to a specific person is a simple but effective way to show you’ve done your homework.

Opening the letter

Your opening paragraph should be short and to the point. State which job you’re applying for and where you saw it advertised. One or two sentences is all you need.

Example: 

‘I am writing to apply for the Part-Time Administrator role, which I saw advertised on Reed.co.uk. Please find my CV attached for your consideration.’

Why are you a good fit for the job?

Now it’s time for your sales pitch. This section is where you highlight the skills and qualifications that make you the perfect candidate. Refer to the job description and match your experience to the requirements.

If you have direct experience, briefly outline it. If you’re changing careers or have less experience, focus on your transferable skills.

Example: 

‘As you will see from my attached CV, I have more than six years of experience in office administration. I believe the knowledge and skills I have developed make me an ideal candidate for this position.’

Why are you looking for part-time work?

This is your chance to briefly explain why you’re seeking a part-time role. You might be studying, raising a family, or looking for a better work-life balance.

Whatever your reason, use this as an opportunity to highlight your flexibility and reliability. This can reassure employers about your commitment and availability. You’re not obligated to share personal details if you’re not comfortable.

Example: 

‘I am currently studying for a diploma and looking for a position that allows me to dedicate time to my course while building my skills. I am very flexible with my hours and happy to take on extra work when possible.’

What can you do for the company?

Use specific, quantifiable examples to show what you can bring to the company. Think about achievements from previous jobs, academic projects, or even volunteer work.

Instead of saying ‘I increased revenue’, try ‘I increased revenue by 15% in six months’. Tangible results are far more impressive to a recruiter.

Example: 

‘In my previous role as a full-time Office Administrator, I was responsible for reorganising the office filing system and implementing a change in stationery supplier, which reduced costs in this area by over 20%.’

Closing the letter

End your letter by restating your interest in the role and your confidence in your ability to succeed. A quick mention of your research into the company can score you extra points.

Finish by thanking the recruiter for their time and stating that you look forward to hearing from them. Use ‘Yours sincerely’ if you know the hiring manager’s name, or ‘Yours faithfully’ if you don’t.

Example: 

‘Thank you for your time and consideration. I am confident that my experience and skills will have an immediate positive impact on your business. I look forward to discussing my application with you further.’

Cover letter examples and samples

Five of the best paying part-time jobs

Tips for writing a part-time job application letter

Ready to start writing? Keep these expert tips in mind to make your cover letter stand out.

1. Always send one

Even if the job description doesn’t ask for a cover letter, sending one can set you apart from other candidates. It shows you’re willing to put in the extra effort and are serious about the role.

2. Keep it concise

Hiring managers are busy people. A short, focused letter that gets straight to the point is much more effective than a long, rambling one. Aim for around three to four paragraphs.

3. Proofread everything

Spelling and grammar mistakes can make you look unprofessional. Always read through your cover letter carefully before sending it. It’s also a good idea to ask a friend or family member to check it for you.

4. Tailor it to the job

A generic, one-size-fits-all cover letter is easy to spot, so it’s important to take the time to customise your letter for each job. Read the job description carefully and show how your skills and experience match what the employer is looking for.

How to: Write a cover letter

Five of the best part-time jobs for students

Ready to find your next part-time job?

A well-crafted part-time cover letter is a great way to stand out from the crowd in your job search. 

By following this advice, you can create a compelling application letter that grabs a recruiter’s attention and helps you land the part-time role you’ve been looking for.

Looking for more inspiration or something specific to your situation? Check out our other cover letter templates:
